content.full_text:
LAW OFFICES OF

NIELSEN, MERKSAMER,
PARRINELLO, MUELLER & NAYLOR
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

SACRAMENTO                          591 REDWOOD HIGHWAY, #4000                    SAN FRANCISCO
770 L STREET, SUITE 800            MILL VALLEY, CALIFORNIA 94941                 650 CALIFORNIA STREET, SUITE 2650
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814       TELEPHONE (415) 389-6800                      SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94108
TELEPHONE (916) 446-6752                                                          TELEPHONE (415) 389-6800


                            August 27, 1992


VIA FACSIMILE & U.S. MAIL

Scott Hallibran, Esq.
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, 8th floor
Sacramento, CA  95814

     Re:  Request for Advice Regarding the Fairness Doctrine

Dear Scott:

     This letter follows up our telephone conversation today
regarding the application of the Political Reform Act to the
provision of free media time by television or radio stations to
committees supporting or opposing ballot measures.  This letter
requests written confirmation of the advice you provided
tentatively over the telephone; namely, that a television or
radio station will make a reportable campaign contribution to the
committee if it provides free media time, because federal law no
longer requires them to do so.

     We represent several committees which are supporting and
opposing statewide ballot measures on the November, 1992 ballot.
Television and radio stations have indicated to some of these
committees that they believe that they must provide them (or
their opponents) with free media time because of the federal
"Fairness Doctrine."

     The FPPC has advised in the past that "free media time
provided under compulsion of the Fairness Doctrine or any rules
or regulations of the Federal Communications Commission is not a
reportable contribution" in California.  (Advice Letter to Graham
Johnson (5/21/90) No. I-90-351 at p. 1; Advice Letter to Richard
Morten (9/14/88) No. A-88-293.)  It based this conclusion on the
fact that the broadcasters were providing the free media time to
comply with federal law, not for political purposes.

     A federal court affirmed the FCC's repeal of the Fairness
Doctrine in Syracuse Peace Council v. WTVH (1987) 867 F.2d 654
(copy enclosed).  Because the Syracuse decision did not involve a
ballot measure, committees and broadcasters have since then not
known for certain whether the broadcasters were still required to
provide free media time to ballot measure committees.  Earlier

Scott Hallibran, Esq.
August 27, 1992
Page 2

this year, the FCC settled this uncertainty in Arkansas AFL-CIO
and The Committee Against Amendment 2 v. Television Station KARK-
TV (1992) 7 FCC Record 541 (copy enclosed), in which it ruled
that the Fairness Doctrine no longer applied to ballot measure
campaigns:

Although our decision in Syracuse did not
deal with ballot issues expressly, we based
our conclusions on the 1985 Fairness Report.
In that Report, the Commission specifically
referred to evidence of how the fairness
doctrine chilled broadcasters' speech with
respect to ballot issues. (Citations.)
Thus, our decision in Syracuse to repeal the
doctrine because of its chilling effect
applies to the fairness doctrine's
application to ballot issues as well.

(Arkansas AFL-CIO, supra; emphasis added.) Although the Arkansas
decision is currently on appeal at the FCC and the 8th Circuit
Court of Appeals, we confirmed with Milton Gross at the FCC that
the decision is still good law.

Because the Fairness Doctrine no longer requires
broadcasters to provide free media time to ballot measure
committees, you advised that broadcasters which choose
nonetheless to give committees free media time would be making a
campaign contribution to the committee. (Gov. Code § 82015.)
This contribution would be reportable by the broadcaster (if they
qualified as a Major Donor) and the committee.

Thank you very much for confirming this advice in writing.
Due to the impending election, we would appreciate a response as
soon as possible.

Sincerely,

[signature]
James R. Sutton

JRS/sfr

Enclosure

6702.01

We represent several committees which are supporting and opposing statewide ballot measures on the November, 1992 ballot. Television and radio stations have indicated to some of these committees that they believe that they must provide them (or their opponents) with free media time because of the federal "Fairness Doctrine." The FPPC has advised in the past that "free media time provided under compulsion of the Fairness Doctrine or any rules or regulations of the Federal Communications Commission is not a reportable contribution" in California. (Advice Letter to Graham Johnson (5/21/90) No. I-90-351 at p. 1; Advice Letter to Richard Morten (9/14/88) No. A-88-293.) It based this conclusion on the fact that the broadcasters were providing the free media time to comply with federal law, not for political purposes. A federal court affirmed the FCC's repeal of the Fairness Doctrine in Syracuse Peace Council v. WTVH (1987) 867 F.2d 654 (copy enclosed). Because the Syracuse decision did not involve a ballot measure, committees and broadcasters have since then not known for certain whether the broadcasters were still required to provide free media time to ballot measure committees. Earlier Scott Hallibran, Esq. August 27, 1992 Page 2 this year, the FCC settled this uncertainty in Arkansas AFL-CIO and The Committee Against Amendment 2 v. Television Station KARK-TV (1992) 7 FCC Record 541 (copy enclosed), in which it ruled that the Fairness Doctrine no longer applied to ballot measure campaigns: Although our decision in Syracuse did not deal with ballot issues expressly, we based our conclusions on the 1985 Fairness Report. In that Report, the Commission specifically referred to evidence of how the fairness doctrine chilled broadcasters' speech with respect to ballot issues. (Citations.) Thus, our decision in Syracuse to repeal the doctrine because of its chilling effect applies to the fairness doctrine's application to ballot issues as well. (Arkansas AFL-CIO, supra; emphasis added.) Although the Arkansas decision is currently on appeal at the FCC and the 8th Circuit Court of Appeals, we confirmed with Milton Gross at the FCC that the decision is still good law.
